domains.summary: The largest domain on the PTCE 2026. Focus on brand/generic pairs, drug classifications, mechanisms of action, indications, side effects, interactions, contraindications, insulin types, storage, and look-alike/sound-alike (LASA) drugs.

sections.title: Top Drug Classes & Suffixes
items: ACE inhibitors (-pril): lisinopril, enalapril, ramipril — hypertension, heart failure. Watch for dry cough, hyperkalemia, angioedema.
ARBs (-sartan): losartan, valsartan, olmesartan — alternative to ACE inhibitors; less cough.
Beta blockers (-olol): metoprolol, atenolol, carvedilol — hypertension, angina, heart failure; don't stop abruptly.
Calcium channel blockers: amlodipine (-dipine), diltiazem, verapamil — hypertension; non-DHP cause bradycardia/constipation.
Statins (-statin): atorvastatin, simvastatin, rosuvastatin — lower LDL; myopathy, ↑LFTs.
Proton pump inhibitors (-prazole): omeprazole, pantoprazole, esomeprazole — GERD, ulcers; take before meals.
H2 blockers (-tidine): famotidine — heartburn.
SSRIs: sertraline, fluoxetine, escitalopram, paroxetine — depression, anxiety; serotonin syndrome risk.
Benzodiazepines (-pam/-lam): lorazepam, alprazolam, diazepam, clonazepam — anxiety, seizures (C-IV controlled).
Fluoroquinolones (-floxacin): ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin — tendon rupture black box, avoid dairy/antacids.
Macrolides (-thromycin): azithromycin, clarithromycin — QTc prolongation.
Tetracyclines (-cycline): doxycycline — photosensitivity, avoid dairy.
DPP-4 (-gliptin), SGLT-2 (-gliflozin), GLP-1 (-glutide): diabetes agents.
Triptans (-triptan): sumatriptan — migraine; CI in CAD.

sections.title: Mechanisms of Action by Class
items: ACE inhibitors: block angiotensin-converting enzyme → ↓ angiotensin II → vasodilation.
ARBs: block angiotensin II receptor.
Statins: inhibit HMG-CoA reductase → ↓ cholesterol synthesis.
PPIs: irreversibly block H+/K+ ATPase proton pump.
Beta blockers: block beta-adrenergic receptors → ↓ HR, ↓ contractility.
Calcium channel blockers: block L-type Ca channels → vasodilation.
Loop diuretics (furosemide): inhibit Na-K-2Cl in loop of Henle.
Warfarin: inhibits vitamin K-dependent clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X).
Heparin: activates antithrombin III.
Metformin: ↓ hepatic glucose production, ↑ insulin sensitivity.
Sulfonylureas: ↑ pancreatic insulin secretion.
SSRIs: block serotonin reuptake.
Triptans: 5-HT1B/1D agonists → cranial vasoconstriction.
MAOIs: block monoamine oxidase → tyramine crisis + serotonin syndrome risk.
Levodopa/carbidopa: dopamine precursor; carbidopa blocks peripheral breakdown.
Allopurinol: inhibits xanthine oxidase → ↓ uric acid production.

sections.title: Common Brand / Generic Pairs
items: Lipitor = atorvastatin
Prinivil/Zestril = lisinopril
Glucophage = metformin
Synthroid = levothyroxine
Prilosec = omeprazole
Norvasc = amlodipine
Coumadin = warfarin
Lasix = furosemide
Zoloft = sertraline
Neurontin = gabapentin
Ventolin/ProAir = albuterol
Zithromax = azithromycin
Eliquis = apixaban
Xarelto = rivaroxaban
Plavix = clopidogrel
Januvia = sitagliptin
Jardiance = empagliflozin
Ozempic/Wegovy = semaglutide
Cymbalta = duloxetine
Wellbutrin = bupropion
Lyrica = pregabalin
Advair = fluticasone/salmeterol
Augmentin = amoxicillin/clavulanate
Flexeril = cyclobenzaprine
Sinemet = carbidopa/levodopa
Zyloprim = allopurinol
Imitrex = sumatriptan

sections.title: Insulin Types
items: Rapid-acting: lispro (Humalog), aspart (NovoLog), glulisine (Apidra) — onset ~15 min; give with meals.
Short-acting: regular (Humulin R, Novolin R) — onset ~30 min; only insulin safe IV.
Intermediate: NPH (Humulin N, Novolin N) — cloudy; peak 4–10 hr; mix last.
Long-acting (basal): glargine (Lantus/Basaglar), detemir (Levemir), degludec (Tresiba) — clear; do NOT mix.
Concentrated: Toujeo (glargine U-300), U-500 regular — NOT interchangeable unit-for-unit.
Storage: unopened refrigerated 36–46°F; do NOT freeze; opened may be kept at room temp for a limited time per product.

sections.title: Common OTC Active Ingredients
items: Tylenol = acetaminophen (max 4,000 mg/day; hepatotoxic in overdose).
Advil/Motrin = ibuprofen (NSAID; take with food).
Aleve = naproxen (NSAID).
Claritin = loratadine; Zyrtec = cetirizine; Allegra = fexofenadine (2nd-gen antihistamines).
Benadryl = diphenhydramine (1st-gen; sedating).
Sudafed = pseudoephedrine (CMEA logbook; ↑ BP; behind counter).
Mucinex = guaifenesin (expectorant); Delsym = dextromethorphan (antitussive).

sections.title: High-Alert & LASA Drugs
items: High-alert (ISMP): insulin, anticoagulants (heparin, warfarin, DOACs), opioids, concentrated electrolytes (KCl), chemotherapy, neuromuscular blockers, methotrexate, digoxin.
LASA examples: hydrOXYzine vs hydrALAZINE, predniSONE vs prednisoLONE, DOPamine vs DOBUTamine, vinBLAStine vs vinCRIStine, Celebrex vs Cerebyx — use tall-man lettering.
Independent double-check required for all high-alert meds.

sections.title: Key Side Effects & Monitoring
items: ACE inhibitors: dry cough, hyperkalemia, angioedema.
Statins: myopathy/rhabdomyolysis, ↑ LFTs.
Metformin: GI upset, lactic acidosis (rare).
Corticosteroids: hyperglycemia, adrenal suppression, osteoporosis, weight gain.
Fluoroquinolones: tendon rupture, QTc, peripheral neuropathy.
Metoclopramide: tardive dyskinesia (black box), EPS.
Amiodarone/pioglitazone: pioglitazone → HF exacerbation, bladder cancer risk.
Bisphosphonates: esophageal irritation — stay upright 30 min, empty stomach.

sections.title: Critical Interactions & Contraindications
items: MAOI + SSRI/SNRI/triptan/tramadol/linezolid/dextromethorphan → serotonin syndrome.
MAOI + tyramine foods (aged cheese, wine, cured meats) → hypertensive crisis.
Warfarin: antidote vitamin K; monitor INR; many CYP + dietary (vitamin K) interactions.
Metformin: hold 48h around IV iodinated contrast (lactic acidosis).
Methotrexate: WEEKLY dosing for RA — daily = fatal error.
Nitroglycerin + PDE-5 inhibitors (sildenafil) → severe hypotension.
St. John's Wort induces CYP3A4 → ↓ OCPs, warfarin, many drugs.
Triptans/ergots: contraindicated in CAD, uncontrolled HTN.
PPIs (omeprazole) ↓ clopidogrel activation (CYP2C19).
Abacavir: HLA-B*5701 test before use (hypersensitivity).

sections.title: Vaccine Storage
items: Live attenuated (MMR, Varicella): FROZEN; CI in pregnancy/immunocompromised.
Inactivated influenza, Shingrix, pneumococcal, Tdap, Hep B: refrigerate 2–8°C.
COVID-19 mRNA: frozen/ultra-cold per product.
Rule: most vaccines refrigerate; MMR/Varicella freeze; NEVER freeze most others.

sections.title: Narrow Therapeutic Index (NTI) Drugs
items: warfarin, digoxin, lithium, phenytoin, theophylline, levothyroxine, carbamazepine, valproic acid, tacrolimus, cyclosporine, aminoglycosides, vancomycin.
Require therapeutic drug monitoring; substitution caution.

sections.title: Known Teratogens (Pregnancy Safety)
items: isotretinoin (iPLEDGE), thalidomide, warfarin, methotrexate, valproic acid, phenytoin, ACE inhibitors/ARBs, statins, tetracyclines, lithium, misoprostol.
REMS programs: iPLEDGE (isotretinoin), clozapine REMS, THALOMID/REVLIMID, TIRF (fentanyl).

domains.summary: Patient Safety and Quality Assurance. Covers error prevention, high-alert meds, ISMP error-prone abbreviations, NDC format, USP compounding standards, hygiene, and reporting.

sections.title: Error Prevention Basics
items: Five rights: right patient, right drug, right dose, right route, right time (+ documentation, reason).
Use 2 identifiers: full name and date of birth.
Barcode scanning verifies the correct product against the order.
Independent double-checks for high-alert meds (e.g., insulin, heparin).
Medication reconciliation compares a patient's meds to new orders to prevent duplication/omission.
Pharmacist performs final verification; a tech cannot verify their own work.

sections.title: ISMP Error-Prone Abbreviations (Avoid)
items: 'U' or 'u' → write 'units' (can look like 0 or 4).
'IU' → write 'international units'.
'QD'/'QOD' → write 'daily'/'every other day'.
Trailing zero (1.0 mg) → write 1 mg.
No leading zero (.5 mg) → write 0.5 mg.
'MS/MSO4/MgSO4' → spell out morphine sulfate or magnesium sulfate.
'μg' → write 'mcg'; 'cc' → write 'mL'; 'D/C' spell out; 'hs' → 'at bedtime'.

sections.title: NDC Format
items: National Drug Code = unique 10- or 11-digit, 3-segment number.
Segment 1 = labeler/manufacturer; Segment 2 = product (strength, dosage form); Segment 3 = package size.
Common formats: 5-4-2, 5-3-2, 4-4-2 (padded to 11 digits with a leading zero in the short segment).

sections.title: Medication Error Types & DUR
items: Types: prescribing, transcription/entry, dispensing, administration, monitoring errors; near-miss = caught before patient.
Adverse drug event (ADE): harm from a drug (may or may not be an error).
Prospective DUR: BEFORE dispensing — interactions, allergies, duplication, dose.
Concurrent DUR: during therapy; Retrospective DUR: after dispensing (audits).
OBRA-90 (effective 1993): mandates prospective DUR + offer to counsel.

sections.title: USP Compounding Standards (effective Nov 1, 2023)
items: USP <795> non-sterile: BUDs — aqueous oral ≤14 days refrigerated, topical ≤30 days, non-aqueous ≤90 days, solids ≤180 days.
USP <797> sterile: Category 1 (ISO 5, short BUD) vs Category 2 (ISO 7 buffer + ISO 8 ante).
PECs: LAFW/CAI for non-hazardous (ISO 5); BSC/CACI for hazardous.
USP <800> hazardous drugs (effective Dec 1, 2023): negative-pressure room, CSTDs, annual training, spill kits.
Gowning order (sterile): shoe covers → hair/beard cover → hand hygiene → gown → mask → sterile gloves.

sections.title: Quality Assurance & Reporting
items: REMS: Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy (e.g., iPLEDGE for isotretinoin, clozapine REMS).
Report errors/near-misses to improve systems (non-punitive culture).
MedWatch: FDA adverse event reporting; FAERS public database; ISMP MERP; VAERS for vaccines.
Root Cause Analysis (RCA): investigates WHY (reactive); FMEA: proactive failure analysis.
PPE donning: gown → mask → goggles → gloves; doffing: gloves → goggles → gown → mask.

domains.summary: Order Entry and Processing. Sig code interpretation, pharmacy calculations, days supply, conversions, prescription intake, refill rules, and insurance processing.

sections.title: Common Sig Codes
items: PO = by mouth; PR = rectally; SL = sublingual; IV/IM/SC = injection routes.
BID = twice daily; TID = 3x daily; QID = 4x daily; QHS = at bedtime; QAM = every morning.
PRN = as needed; ac = before meals; pc = after meals; q6h = every 6 hours.
gtt = drop; OD = right eye; OS = left eye; OU = both eyes; AD/AS/AU = right/left/both ears.
ii tab = 2 tablets; i cap = 1 capsule; qs = quantity sufficient; UD = as directed.

sections.title: Key Conversions
items: 1 tsp = 5 mL; 1 tbsp = 15 mL; 1 fl oz = 30 mL; 1 cup = 240 mL.
1 grain (gr) ≈ 65 mg; 1 oz = 28.4 g; 1 g = 1,000 mg; 1 mg = 1,000 mcg.
1 gallon = 3,785 mL; 1 pint = 473 mL; 1 quart = 946 mL.
Temperature: °C = (°F − 32) × 5/9; body 37°C = 98.6°F; fridge 2–8°C = 36–46°F.

sections.title: Core Calculations
items: Days supply = total quantity ÷ amount used per day.
Percent strength: X% = X grams per 100 mL (or 100 g).
Dilution: C1V1 = C2V2.
IV flow rate (mL/hr) = total volume ÷ total time (hr).
Drops/min = (volume × drop factor) ÷ time in minutes.
Alligation: parts method for mixing two strengths to an intermediate strength.
Powder volume = final volume − diluent added.
Dose by weight = mg/kg × weight (kg).

sections.title: Prescription Intake
items: Verify patient identifiers: full name and date of birth.
Confirm drug, strength, dosage form, quantity, directions, prescriber, and DEA (if controlled).
Check insurance/DUR and allergies before adjudication.
Technicians enter data; the pharmacist performs the final clinical verification.

sections.title: Refill Rules
items: C-II: NO refills; Rx typically valid 30 days (state may vary).
C-III–C-V: up to 5 refills within 6 months of written date.
Non-controlled: per state law, commonly up to 1 year.
C-II partial fill: remainder within 72 hr (community) or 60 days (LTCF/terminally ill).

sections.title: Insurance / Third-Party Processing
items: BIN routes the claim; PCN sub-routing; Group ID and Member ID identify plan/patient.
DAW codes: 0 = no instruction, 1 = prescriber brand, 2 = patient brand.
COB = coordination of benefits (primary vs secondary).
Reject codes: 70 = not covered, 75 = prior auth required, 76 = plan limits/refill too soon, 25 = invalid prescriber ID.

sections.title: Auxiliary Labels
items: Take with food: NSAIDs, metformin, iron. Empty stomach: levothyroxine, bisphosphonates.
Avoid alcohol: metronidazole, CNS depressants. Avoid sun: doxycycline, fluoroquinolones, sulfonamides.
May cause drowsiness: opioids, benzos, antihistamines. Shake well: suspensions.
Do not crush/chew: ER/XL/XR/SR, enteric-coated. Refrigerate/do not freeze: insulin, amoxicillin suspension.

domains.summary: Federal Requirements. DEA controlled substance schedules, DEA forms, refill rules, key laws (CSA, HIPAA, OBRA-90, PPPA, CMEA, DSCSA), and DEA number verification.

sections.title: DEA Controlled Substance Schedules
items: C-I: no accepted medical use, high abuse (heroin, LSD, marijuana federally).
C-II: high abuse, accepted use — NO refills (oxycodone, hydrocodone, morphine, Adderall, fentanyl, methadone).
C-III: moderate abuse (Tylenol #3, testosterone, buprenorphine) — up to 5 refills / 6 months.
C-IV: lower abuse (alprazolam, lorazepam, tramadol, zolpidem, carisoprodol) — up to 5 refills / 6 months.
C-V: lowest abuse (cough syrups with codeine, pregabalin) — up to 5 refills / 6 months.
Tramadol → C-IV (2014); hydrocodone combos → C-II (Oct 6, 2014).

sections.title: DEA Forms
items: Form 222: order/transfer Schedule I & II substances (electronic = CSOS); triplicate, no alterations, ship within 60 days, keep copy 3 for 2 years.
Form 106: report theft or significant loss of controlled substances (within 1 business day).
Form 41: record destruction/disposal of controlled substances.
Form 224: pharmacy application for DEA registration (224a = renewal every 3 years).
CSOS (authorized 2005): electronic ordering of C-I/C-II; requires DEA digital certificate.

sections.title: Key Federal Laws
items: FDCA (1938): FDA drug safety authority; Durham-Humphrey (1951): Rx vs OTC classification.
Kefauver-Harris (1962): required proof of efficacy (thalidomide disaster).
Controlled Substances Act (1970): created the 5-schedule system; Poison Prevention Packaging Act (1970): child-resistant containers.
OBRA-90 (effective 1993): offer to counsel + prospective DUR.
HIPAA (1996; Privacy 2003, Security 2005): protects patient PHI.
CMEA (2005): pseudoephedrine limits 3.6 g/day, 9 g/30 days; logbook + photo ID.
DATA 2000: office-based buprenorphine for OUD; DSCSA (2013): track-and-trace (full enforcement Nov 2024).
Biennial (every 2 years) controlled substance inventory required.

sections.title: HIPAA — Tech Specifics
items: PHI = any info identifying the patient (name, DOB, Rx number, diagnosis).
Minimum necessary standard; Notice of Privacy Practices at first fill.
Permitted disclosures: treatment, payment, operations (TPO) without extra authorization.
Breach notification within 60 days; penalties civil ($100–$50,000/violation) and criminal.

sections.title: PPPA & Pseudoephedrine (CMEA)
items: PPPA (1970): child-resistant packaging for Rx and certain OTC; patient may waive in writing.
PPPA exemptions: SL nitroglycerin, SL isosorbide, prednisone ≤105 mg, certain peds antibiotics.
CMEA: 3.6 g/day, 9 g/30 days pseudoephedrine; photo ID + logbook; kept behind counter.

sections.title: DEA Number Verification
items: Format: 2 letters + 7 digits. First letter = registrant type (B = physician, C = pharmacy, M = mid-level); second letter = registrant's last-name initial.
Check digit math: (1st+3rd+5th digits) + 2×(2nd+4th+6th digits) = sum; the last digit of the sum must equal the 7th (check) digit.
Example: AB1234563 → (1+3+5)=9; (2+4+6)=12 ×2 =24; 9+24=33; last digit 3 = check digit 3. Valid.

sections.title: Recalls & Orange Book
items: FDA recalls: Class I (serious/death), Class II (temporary/reversible), Class III (minor/labeling).
Orange Book codes: A/AB = therapeutically equivalent (substitutable); B = not equivalent.
NPI: 10-digit provider ID (effective 2007); required on claims; does NOT replace DEA number.
Rule of thumb: always follow the STRICTER of state vs federal law.
